coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional photothermal effect to promote band bending and water oxidation kinetics for improving photoelectrochemical water splitting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Jiajia
Tang, Xiangxuan
Liu, Cunxing
Li, Yongjun
Kong, Lingna
Wang, Jiansheng
Li, Haijin
Xie, Qian
Wang, Jianmin
Li, Song
coredata.dc:description: The photothermal effect is proved to favor the photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation, but the impact of the photothermal effect on the band bending in the heterojunction has seldom been revealed. In this work, we constructed a Z schematic heterojunction by heteroatoms doping, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was anchored to trigger the local heat under the NIR irradiation. With the photothermal effect, the photocurrent density of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 reached 1.02 mA/cm2 (1.23 VRHE), 2.30 mA/cm2 (1.60 VRHE), which yielded an increased by 1.3 folds, 1.7 folds, respectively. Detailed studies showed that the photothermal effect would enlarge the contact potential difference in the heterojunction and band bending at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface (SEI). Furthermore, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics was also accelerated, which was evidenced by the Tafel slope decreasing from 88 mV/decade to 78 mV/decade. Despite the photovoltage being reduced, the advantages of the photothermal effect still out weighted the disadvantages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal effect
Band bending
Heteroatom doping
Heterojunction
Photoelectrochemistry

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of PEC water splitting performances under thermal effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart of the preparation of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi photoanode.
(a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) SAED pattern of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi
(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman pattern, and fine XPS spectra of (c) Fe2p, (d) Ti2p, (e) Zn 2p, (f) O1s, (g) C1s, (h) Co 2p and (i) P 2p for (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi
(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra, (b) LSV curves, (c) I-t curves under chopped light at 1.23 VRHE of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3 and (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi
(a) Charge separation efficiency, (b) charge injection efficiency under the illumination of 1 Sun with or without NIR.
The temperature variation of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi under 10 W illumination with a distance of 5 cm, (b) Tafel plots under 1 Sun illumination with or without NIR.
the open circuit potentials and (b) photovoltages that were measured under different light switching, (c) the lifetime of carriers that derived from the OCPs.
(a) Nyquist plots under illumination, the inset is the equivalent circuit, and (b) Mott-Schottky plots.
The band alignment of the samples, (a) before contact, (b) equilibrium under 1 Sun illumination, (c) (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi under AM 1.5 and NIR illumination.
Carrier density, flat band potential, and onset potentials.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional photothermal effect to promote band bending and water oxidation kinetics for improving photoelectrochemical water splitting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photothermal effect is proved to favor the photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation, but the impact of the photothermal effect on the band bending in the heterojunction has seldom been revealed. In this work, we constructed a Z schematic heterojunction by heteroatoms doping, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was anchored to trigger the local heat under the NIR irradiation. With the photothermal effect, the photocurrent density of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 reached 1.02 mA/cm2 (1.23 VRHE), 2.30 mA/cm2 (1.60 VRHE), which yielded an increased by 1.3 folds, 1.7 folds, respectively. Detailed studies showed that the photothermal effect would enlarge the contact potential difference in the heterojunction and band bending at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface (SEI). Furthermore, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics was also accelerated, which was evidenced by the Tafel slope decreasing from 88 mV/decade to 78 mV/decade. Despite the photovoltage being reduced, the advantages of the photothermal effect still out weighted the disadvantages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen production from photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting provides a prospective alternative to address the environmental problems and alleviate the energy crisis. The transient metal oxides, such as α-Fe2O3, TiO2, and BiVO4, have been emerging as the most studied photoanodes, the empty d or sp orbitals of which form the bottom of the conduction band, and thus show tunable optical, and electrical properties by precisely controlling the composition of the photoanodes. However, the developments of these photoanodes are greatly restricted by their inherent serious recombination, low mobility, short hole diffusion length, and sluggish water oxidation kinetics [1]. To address these problems, the routine strategies of heterojunction, surface modification, and morphology design are developed.

Recently, the significant advantage of the photothermal effect on PEC water splitting has been confirmed [2,3]. Ye et al. find that elevated temperature would prolong the hole diffusion length [4]. Liu et al. reveal that charge separation efficiencies are enhanced both in the bulk and on the surface under the photothermal effect [2,5]. Cao et al. find the thermal effect not only stimulates the small polaron activity but also accelerates the charge transfer and reaction dynamics. On the contrary, the photothermal effect has also been reported to generally lower the energy conversion efficiency in PEC and photovoltaic cells by reducing the photovoltage and band bending at the interface of semiconductor and electrolyte (SEI) [6–8], which would lower the driving forces for the charge separation and water oxidation. However, this effect has not yet been reported for photoanode, especially in the heterojunction, which is involved in two semiconductors.

Z-schematic heterojunction, which affords efficient charge separation by the built-in electric field and retains the strong redox potential of photocarriers, is proposed to enhance the PEC performance [9]. For efficient charge separation, in addition to the suitable band alignment, a good match of dielectric constant, lattice, and grain boundaries between two semiconductors is also essential [10], which remains a challenge in material selection [11]. Due to the excellent light absorption properties, high theoretical photocurrent density (12.9 mA/cm2), and adjustable band structure, α-Fe2O3 is widely believed to be a promising photoanode. Notably, its band alignment is tunable by foreign element doping. For example, the Zn is reported to elevate the valence band of the α-Fe2O3 [12,13], while the CBM of Ti-doped hematite is inclined to shift toward the valence band [14]. As a result, the doping of α-Fe2O3 using heteroatoms holds promise for effectively enhancing charge separation efficiency by matching favorable energy levels and improving carrier concentration. Additionally, such a Z-scheme quasi-homojunction can alleviate the interface defects or mismatches [11]. But, it is still scarce to study the influence of the photothermal effect on the Z-schematic heterojunction for boosting the efficiency of PEC.

Herein, we demonstrated a quasi-homojunction of the Z-scheme based on the α-Fe2O3 can be prepared by heteroatoms doping via a two-step hydrothermal method. The photothermal effect is triggered by the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) under near-infrared laser irradiation (NIR, 808 nm), and the surface temperature of the photoanode was elevated to 36 oC, which was 10 oC higher than the electrolyte. Under the photothermal effect, the contact potential difference at the interface of heterojunction was increased, which can strengthen the built-in field as well as the charge separation in the bulk. Meanwhile, the PEC impedance spectra that proved the band bending at SEI was also elevated, which was consistent with the theoretical analysis. Additionally, the Tafel plots confirmed the OER kinetics was accelerated, and the charge separation on the SEI was improved although the photovoltage was reduced. This work not just deepens the understanding of the photothermal effect on the band bending in the heterojunction, but illuminates the possible strategy to advance the performance of the PEC water oxidation.
All the reagents used in this work were analytical reagent grade without further purification, and deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The preparation procedure was depicted in Fig. 1 . The FTO (F doped SnO2) was used as the substrate, and they were washed with acetone, ethanol, and ethanol for 20min, 20 min, and 20 min, respectively. The Ti, Zn co-doped α-Fe2O3 ((Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3) were prepared by the hydrothermal method according to the literature [15,16]. Briefly, the stock solution including 0.0375 mol/L FeCl3·6H2O, 0.025 mol/L Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, and 0.0375 mol/L NaNO3 was stirred for 15 min. Next, the 50–70 μL of 0.05% TiCl4 (volume fraction) aqueous solution was added. The mixture was poured into the 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclaves and kept at 100 oC for 5 h in the oven, cooled down naturally. Then the samples were calcined at 550 oC for 2 h, and 700 oC for 20 min. Since then, the samples were immersed in 1 mol/L NaOH overnight to remove the excessive ZnO. In the homojunction of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3, the Ti-doped α-Fe2O3 was also prepared by the hydrothermal method, but the solution was a mixture of 0.0375 mol/L FeCl3·6H2O, 0.0375 mol/L NaNO3 and 1 mL 0.05% TiCl4 (volume fraction), the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3 film was used as the substrate. The remaining steps followed the preparation of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3.

(Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 was immersed in commercial carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of 2mg/mL for a few hours. Then it was taken out and dried by the N2 flow. Finally, the Co-Pi was photo-assisted electrodeposited at 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl in a solution of 0.25 mmol/L Co(NO3)2 and 0.1 mol/L K2HPO4, under air mass (AM) 1.5 G illumination, the samples named (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi [17].

The films were characterized by the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu Kα = 0.15406 nm, Bruker D8 ADVANCE), field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEM-7001F), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL2100). The element mapping was analyzed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The Raman spectroscopy (λExcitation = 532 nm) was conducted with the Renishaw inVia Raman microscope. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermal Scientific) using Al Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV, 15 kV) was used to examine the sample chemical composition, and the XPS spectra were calibrated using the C 1s peak (284.6 eV) and fitted by the XPSPEAK (Version 41). The optical properties of the samples were investigated by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General Instrument), and the wavelength ranged from 200 to 800 nm at a rate of 2 nm/s.

All photoelectrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI760E (Chen Hua, ShangHai) electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system. The prepared samples, Pt mesh, and Ag/AgCl (3.5 M KCl) were operated as the working electrode, auxiliary electrode, and reference electrode. The illumination density of the light was AM 1.5 100 mW cm−2. The infrared laser of 808 nm (MDL-N) with a power of 3 W was used, the distance between the photoanode and the NIR lamp was about 5 cm, and the irradiation area of NIR was 3.14 cm2. The surface temperature was monitored by the camera of Testo 868. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), chronoamperometry (I-t), applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed. The EIS was measured between 10 kHz and 0.1 Hz with 10 mV amplitude perturbation, and the Mott-Schottky (MS) spectra were obtained at a frequency of 1 kHz in the range of 0.6–1.6 VRHE. The open circuit potentials were also collected both in the dark and under 1 sun illumination. The electrolyte in the above PEC tests was 1 M NaOH. Additionally, the efficiency of charge separation both in the bulk and on the surface was analyzed, and the electrolyte was 0.5 M H2O2 + 1 M NaOH [18], details can be seen in the supporting information. In this work, all the potentials were scaled to the RHE unless stated otherwise.

Fig. S1(a)–(d) shows that (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3, (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3, and (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi display the similar nanoparticle morphology, and the statistic diameter of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi is ca. 90 nm with the length of ca.220 nm. The TEM in Fig. 2 (a) of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi suggests a similar size distribution to the SEM observation, and the HRTEM in Fig. 2(b) shows the lattice fringes of 0.368 nm, which can be indexed to the (012) of α-Fe2O3. The amorphous edge proves the presence of the Co-Pi, and no detectable signal of CQD can be attributable to the size being beyond the detection limit. Additionally, the selected area diffraction pattern in Fig. 2(c) could be indexed as the single crystal Fe2O3 with the incident electron beam along [00-1], suggesting the Zn, Ti doping does not change its crystal structure. Importantly, the elemental mapping shown in Fig. S2 provides seven elements of Fe, O, Zn, Ti, C, P, and Co, which are evenly distributed and confirm the uniform distribution of CQDs and Co-Pi.

Moreover, the crystalline structures are characterized by XRD. Fig. 3 (a) shows that diffraction peaks at 2θ = 33.15°, 35.61°, and 63.98° belong to the (104), (110), and (300) planes of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS NO. 33–664), the residual peaks are attributed to the SnO2 from the substrate. No other impurity peaks appear, evidencing the Zn and Ti are successfully doped into the α-Fe2O3. Further coating the CQDs and Co-Pi does not influence the crystalline structure, which can be due to the low amount of CQDs and amorphous-crystal of Co-Pi. Additionally, the Raman spectra are gathered to confirm the structural information. In Fig. S3, both the samples exhibit the peaks that belong to α-Fe2O3 with A1g mode (225 cm−1, 502 cm−1), Eg mode (247 cm−1, 294 cm−1, 410 cm−1, 612 cm−1). The peak of ∼660 cm−1 is derived from the lattice-disordered structure (LO), which is caused by the introduction of the heteroatoms of Ti, and Zn. Notably, Fig. 3(b) displays the Raman peak at 1317 cm−1 that originated from the second-order scattering process in Fe2O3 [19], which overlaps with the sp3 hybrids of CQD (D band), the emergence of an extra Raman peak at 1596 cm−1 (sp2 hybrids, G band) further proves the successful loading of the CQD on the heterojunction.

XPS measurements are also used to explore the chemical composition and all the binding energies (BEs) are calibrated to the C1s peak at 284.6 eV. The full spectrum in Fig. S4 illustrates the presence of the Fe, O, Zn, Ti, C, Co, P in the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi. Fig. 3(c) shows that the Fe 2p XPS exhibits two main peaks at 724.8 eV, and 710.4 eV, with satellite peaks at 712.1 eV and 718.5 eV [20], suggesting the main existence of Fe3+ in the composite, which is consistent with the previous reports for α-Fe2O3 [12]. Peaks at 709.2 eV and 722.9 eV show the state of Fe2+, the mixture of Fe2+ and Fe3+ is induced by the Zn, Ti doping. Fig. 3(d) exhibits the peaks at BEs of 457.6 eV and 463.3 eV, which are considered to be Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 for Ti4+. While the Zn 2p spectrum exists at shoulder peaks at 1021.2 eV and 1044.1 eV corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, which are consistent with the presence of Zn2+ [21](Fig. 3(e)). As shown in Fig. 3(f), the O1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks, the BEs of 529.27 eV、531.16 eV, and 535.30 eV correspond to the lattice oxygen (Fe3+-O), oxygen vacancies (resulted from the Ti, Zn doping) and chemisorbed –OH. The characteristic peaks located at BEs = ca. 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.7 eV, which belong to C–C, C–O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3(g)), evidence the presence of the CQDs [22]. In Fig. 3(h)–(i), the BEs at 781.0 eV and 786.24 are assigned to the Co2+ and satellite of Co2+ [23], and the BE at 132.8 eV corresponds to PO4 3−, proving the successful deposition of Co-Pi on the surface of the heterojunction [24]. All the above XPS analyses validate the successful preparation of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of PEC water splitting performances under thermal effect
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis is used to probe the effect of CQD and Co-Pi on the optical absorbance behavior and band gap of the heterojunction. Fig. 4 (a) shows both the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 and (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi is similar with light absorbance between 300 and 800 nm. Notably, due to the anchored CQD and Co-Pi, the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi slightly elevates the absorbance than the bare heterojunction [22]. Besides, the bandgaps are also derived from the Tauc plots according to equation S1, as illustrated in Fig. S5. The derived E g is 1.97 eV and 1.98 eV for (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 and (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi, respectively, indicating that the anchored CQDs and Co-Pi have little effect on the bandgap of heterojunction [22].

To evaluate the photothermal effect of CQDs on the PEC performance of the heterojunction, the photocurrent density, onset potential, stability, applied bias photon-to-current conversion efficiency (ABPE), and charge separation efficiency are characterized. Fig. 4(b) shows the J1.23VRHE reaches 0.64 mA/cm2 for (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3, which shows an abrupt increase when compared to (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3 (0.34 mA/cm2) and Ti–Fe2O3 (0.42 mA/cm2), evidencing the effective charge separation in the heterojunction (Fig. S6). Further modified with Co-Pi and CQDs would enhance the photocurrent density to 0.79 mA/cm2, which yields up to 1.3 folds (1.02 mA/cm2) under the photothermal effect. Additionally, the onset potentials are also derived from the LSV curves by plotting the J2 vs potential. As shown in Fig. S7, the Von of heterojunction negatively shifts from 1.04 VRHE to 0.96 VRHE after anchoring the cocatalyst of Co-Pi, and it further reduces to 0.87 VRHE under the photothermal effect, confirming the accelerated OER. In Fig. S8, the ABPE of each sample is calculated based on equation S2. The (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi under photothermal effect shows the highest ABPE of 0.083% (1.09 VRHE), up to 2.24 times the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@ Ti–Fe2O3. And the sequence of the maximum ABPE for samples is consistent with the LSVs [42,43].

To understand the importance of the photothermal effect, the transient photocurrents under chopped light at 1.23 VRHE are collected. As Fig. 4(c) shows, the anodic transient photocurrent is attributed to the photogenerated holes which initially reach the surface. However, due to the presence of the surface states or the recombination with the photoelectrons, the transient current decays to the steady value associated with water oxidation (faradaic current). The ratio of δ = (Jtransient– Jsteady)/Jtransient is employed to evaluate the charge separation behavior on the surface, it shows the δ of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 and (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi are equivalent (8%) [25]. Notably, under the photothermal effect, the δ greatly decreases to 3%, evidencing the recombination is suppressed. Meanwhile, the steady current densities in I-t are in high accordance with the previous LSV.

To quantify the photothermal effect on the charge separation, the charge separation efficiency (ηSep) and the injection efficiency (ηInj) are also calculated by probing the photocurrent density both in the NaOH and NaOH + H2O2 according to equations (S3) - (S4), as shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. S9 and equations (S5) - (S6) describe the calculation details of the theoretical photocurrent density. In Fig. 5(a), the ηSep also increases from 11.6% to 12.9% under the photothermal effect at a potential of 1.23 VRHE, indicating the photothermal effect slightly improves the charge separation in the bulk. While, the ηInj increases to 77.9% from 64.0% with the assistance of the CQDs and Co-Pi, owing to the OER catalytic effect of Co-Pi. And ηInj abruptly to nearly 100% all over the measured potential for (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti– Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi under the photothermal effect (Fig. 5(b)), can be attributed to the activated intermediates species and the further accelerated OER kinetics. The results described above proved that the photothermal effect can enhance the photocarrier separation efficiency both in the bulk and on the surface, simultaneously.

To reveal the photothermal effect on the surface reaction, the kinetics of water oxidation was evaluated. Firstly, the steady surface temperature (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi is recorded by the IR camera, and the surface temperature of the sample is 10 oC higher than the electrolyte (Fig. 6 (a)) during the measurements. As it is well known, water oxidation is an endothermic reaction, while higher reaction temperature favors higher yield. Fig. 6(b) displays that the Tafel slope is significantly reduced from 136 mV/dec to 88 mV/dec with coating CQDs and Co-Pi owing to the catalytic effect. It continues to decrease to 78 mV/dec under the photothermal effect, indicating the accelerated OER kinetics, which agrees well with the theoretical predictions.

Fig. 5(a) and (b) confirm the photothermal effect affects not only the water oxidation on the surface but also charge transport in the bulk. However, the latter effect has seldom been clarified in PEC water oxidation. Herein, we first analyze the effect of photothermal on the charge separation efficiency in the heterojunction. The contact potential difference (VD) of the built-in field between two semiconductors in the heterojunction can be described according to equation (1) [26,27].
(1)

V D

E
F n 2

E

represents the fermi level of the Ti–Fe2O3, (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3, respectively. The equilibrated electron concentrations in the two semiconductors are depicted as
n
n 01
and
n

,
k 0
equals to the Boltzmann constant.
(2)
n

(4)

Because the Ti–Fe2O3, (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 are derived from the Ti and Ti, Zn co-doping of the α-Fe2O3, then the
n i
equals to the intrinsic carrier density of α-Fe2O3. The
n

is 0.5 × 1019 cm−3 and 1.52 × 1019 cm−3 for Ti–Fe2O3 and (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3, respectively, as shown in Fig. S10 and Table S1. Equation (1) is transformed into equation (5):
(5)

)

We can then conclude that the VD is proportional to the temperature, indicating an increase in the temperature would induce an enhancement in VD, which can strengthen the band bending in the space charge layer at the interface of heterojunction and improve the charge separation, which aligns with the charge separation efficiency in Fig. 5(b).

Secondly, the open circuit potential measurements are also performed to evaluate the effect of photothermal on the charge transport in the bulk of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi by switching the NIR and AM 1.5 separately. Fig. 7 (a) displays that the OCP in the dark remains almost constant, ca. 890 mVRHE. Under individual AM 1.5 illumination, the OCP drops to 679 mVRHE with the photovoltage (Vph) of 206 mV. However, once the local heat is triggered by the photothermal effect, it would result in a decreased Vph by ca. 20 mV, which is detrimental to the water oxidation [6] and the details will be discussed later. Besides, the transient decay of OCP is used to calculate the carrier lifetime according to Eq. (6) [28–30].
(6)
τ n
= −
k B
T
e
(
d O C P
d t
)
− 1
Where, τn, k B, T, e, are the potential-dependent carrier lifetime, Boltzmann's constant, absolute temperature (K), and the elementary charge, respectively. Because the decay of the carriers is dependent on the spatial charges at the transient time when the light is turned off, the faster the decay kinetics, the stronger the built-in electric field (at SEI) is. Notably, equation (6) depicts that elevating T would result in slow decay kinetics, indicating a weaker built-in electric field. And Fig. 7(c) displays the τn is in the magnitude of seconds (18 s, 54 s), or the micro-second (98 ms) with photothermal effect ON/OFF, validating the photothermal effect would weaken the built-in electric field at SEI and give rise to charge recombination [31–34].

The Nyquist plots obtained under illumination and in the dark are used to explore the charge transport and transfer behaviors in the bulk and at the SEI. Fig. 8 (a), and Fig. S11 show the typical Nyquist plots fitted by the equivalent circuit. It is composed of the series resistance of the solution, Rs, the Rct in the low-frequency corresponds to the charge transfer resistance at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface. While the constant phase element (CPE) refers to the space charge capacitance. The drastically decreased radius is found after decorating the Co-Pi and CQD, which is attributable to their high OER catalytic property [22]. Importantly, Table S2 shows, the Rct obtained from the Nyquist plots in the dark is much bigger than that under illumination, suggesting the slower charge transfer, which can be attributed to the absence of the extra charge driving force from the photovoltage. While under illumination, especially under the photothermal effect, the Rct possesses the smallest radius, confirming the effectiveness of charge transfer that resulted from the decreased activation barrier for OER [5]. Moreover, Rct of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi is smaller than the heterojunction, which is contributed by the increased donor density. The Rct reaches the minimum under NIR, which can be due to the charge transport can be accelerated by elevating the temperature [4], which is consistent with the sequence of the ηSep in Fig. 5(b). Additionally, under illumination, the decoration of the OER catalyst of Co-Pi would enhance the capacitance due to the prompted charge transfer behavior [35]. When the NIR is on, the capacitance is reduced, which may owe to the steepened band bending and enlarged space charge layer. And further research is required to explore the relationship between the photothermal effect and capacitance.

To deepen understanding of the superior PEC water oxidation on the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi under NIR irradiation, the band alignments of the samples are investigated by Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots. As expected, positive slopes indicate that all samples are n-type semiconductors and Ti, Zn doping has no influence on electron transition (Fig. 8(b) and Fig. S10). The donor density (ND) derived from the slope of the linear region according to equation S (6)–S (7) is listed in Table 1 . Under photothermal effect, Nd of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3@C&Co-Pi is the highest (5.88 × 1019cm−3), this is expected due to the extra thermally excited carriers [4,5,8].

To further explore the mechanism of the enhanced PEC water oxidation performance, the interfaces ((Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3, semiconductor-electrolyte) properties under the photothermal affected are discussed. Herein, we mainly focused on the band alignment, which is firstly proposed based on the Eg from the Tauc plots and flat band potential from MS plots. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), before contact, Ef, n1, Ef, n2 represent the quasi-Fermi level of electrons in (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3, Ti–Fe2O3, respectively, and the quasi-Fermi levels of electrons and holes (Ep, n1, Ep, n2) coincide. The resulting band alignment and band bending at the interface are illustrated in Fig. 9(b), proving the successful preparation of a Z schematic heterojunction. Under illumination, the electron equilibrates among the semiconductors and the electrolyte, but the quasi-Fermi level of holes would split, and the energy gap between the quasi-Fermi level of electrons and holes is defined as Vph. Thanks to the built-in electric field, the photo holes on (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3 would recombine with the photoelectrons on the Ti–Fe2O3, improving the charge separation efficiency. Also, the band bending at the SEI would drive the photo holes to oxidize water with an overpotential of η1.

The photothermal effect impact the interfacial properties in four aspects and is further depicted in Fig. 9(c), firstly, the contact potential difference between the (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 is enhanced, thus the charge separation efficiency in the bulk can be elevated. Secondly, according to the negative flat-potential (M − S), the quasi-Femi level of electrons is more negative than in the other circumstances, increasing band bending at the SEI, which would provide a stronger driving force for oxidizing water. Thirdly, the Vph at the SEI decreases, which is detrimental to the PEC performance. Finally, the OER process is activated by the photothermal with the overpotential reducing to η2, meanwhile, the kinetics is also accelerated. It is obvious that the pros of the photothermal effect outweighed the cons, and sheds new light on the impact of the photothermal effect on the bend bending in photoanode, not just the surface reaction.

In summary, we have prepared a Z-scheme heterojunction of (Ti, Zn)–Fe2O3@Ti–Fe2O3 by manipulating the band alignment of α-Fe2O3 via heteroatoms doping, studied and unraveled the photothermal effect on the PEC water oxidation. Due to the local heat triggered by the CQD under NIR irradiation, the photocurrent density increased to 1.02 mA/cm2 (1.23 VRHE), which increased by 1.3 folds than the same sample without the photothermal effect. Also, the onset potential shifted negatively to 0.87 VRHE. The photothermal effect's impact on the PEC performance can be understood in the following way. Firstly, the contact potential difference in the heterojunction was enlarged, by which the strengthened built-in field can enhance the charge separation efficiency in the bulk. Secondly, the increased band bending at the SEI provided a stronger driving force for the OER although the photovoltage was reduced. The OER kinetics was accelerated, evidenced by the Tafel slope decreasing from 88 mV/decade to 78 mV/decade. This work provides a deepened understanding of the photothermal effect on PEC water oxidation and broadens the research strategy for constructing more efficient photoanodes.

Jiajia Cai: Writing – review & editing. Xiangxuan Tang: Data curation. Cunxing Liu: Formal analysis. Yongjun Li: Methodology. Lingna Kong: Methodology. Jiansheng Wang: Formal analysis. Haijin Li: Resources. Qian Xie: Writing – review & editing. Jianmin Wang: Supervision. Song Li: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
